summary: I joined the International Longshore and Warehouse Union in 1968 after working at Shell Chemical for ten years. My brothers were already members, one in Local 63 and the other in Local 13, and they helped me get started. I worked as a clerk and also did longshore work for a short period. I was involved during significant events like the 1971 strike and witnessed the 2002 lockout, although I retired just before that.

Before joining the ILWU, I worked for Shell Chemical for ten years. I was very active in the Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union there and served as secretary of our local group. I often clashed with the company during that time. It was demanding work, handling heavy 500-pound drums, which was tough physically.

entity: Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union
summary: While working at Shell Chemical, I was very active in the Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union. Our plant was 100 percent unionized, and I served as secretary of the group for several years. I was involved in union activities and had frequent clashes with the company before eventually leaving to work on the waterfront.

I grew up in Grass Valley, California, where my father was a gold miner for 55 years. I have fond memories of the area and the small towns nearby like Nevada City. The Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad, which ran from Colfax to Nevada City, was something I often saw as a child and later wrote a poem about it in the early 1970s.

I served in the United States Army from 1954 to 1955. After my discharge, I went to Oregon to work with my brother in logging. Later, during my time as a longshoreman, I worked at the army dock, banding and tying motorcycles for shipment overseas, which was a demanding job.

After my military service, I moved to Oregon in 1955 to work in the logging industry with my brother Ken near Glendale. I worked as a choker setter behind him for about a year until the woods closed down, which was common in Oregon logging. After that, I moved to Southern California to join my other brothers and eventually got into the longshore union.

I worked out of Glendale, Oregon, in the logging business for about a year after leaving the Army. I was setting chokers behind my brother who drove the Caterpillar machinery. When the logging operations shut down there, I had to look for work elsewhere, which led me to Southern California.

Southern California became my home after the logging work in Oregon ended. Both my brothers were working in the longshore union here—one in Local 63 and the other in Local 13—and they helped me get a job at Shell Chemical and later into the clerks union in 1968. I worked at Wilmington and San Pedro, and I experienced important union events like the 1971 strike while living here.

entity: International Longshoremen's and Warehousemen's Union Local 13
summary: My brother Chuck was a member of ILWU Local 13, and he helped me get started on the waterfront in Southern California. He worked in Stockton for three years after high school and shared stories about Harry Bridges and the union. I also worked alongside Local 13 members during my early days as a longshoreman and clerk.

entity: International Longshoremen's and Warehousemen's Union Local 63
summary: My brother Raymond was a clerk in ILWU Local 63, and he helped me get into the clerks union in 1968 by assisting me with the test and introducing me to key people. I worked as a clerk at Local 63, including at the Matson Company in Wilmington. Although I was never active in union politics, I was present during major events like the 1971 strike.

I grew up in Northern California, specifically Grass Valley, where my father was a gold miner for many years. I also worked in lumber companies in Northern California before joining the Army. The region and its small towns, like Nevada City and Colfax, hold a special place in my memories, and I wrote poetry about the local narrow gauge railroad there.

My brother Chuck worked in Stockton for three years after finishing high school in 1949. He often talked about his experiences working on ships there and about Harry Bridges, which gave me some early knowledge of the ILWU. Stockton was part of the background that influenced my eventual move into the longshore union.

I first heard about Harry Bridges through my brother Chuck, who worked on ships in Stockton and spoke about him often. Bridges was a legendary figure in the ILWU, and his story was part of what made me think the waterfront would be a good place to work. Though I never met him personally, his influence was felt through the union culture I joined.

I started working as a clerk at Matson, Inc. in 1968 at Berth 198 in Wilmington, California. On my first day, I met Morey Segal, who was finishing his career as a B-man before moving into real estate. My job involved checking cargo as trucks were loaded, and it was a relatively easy start compared to other work I had done.

Wilmington, Los Angeles, was where I worked as a clerk starting in 1968, specifically at the Matson Company at Berth 198. It was also the site of my picket duty during the 1971 ILWU strike at Berth 146. Wilmington was a central place in my longshore career and union experiences.

San Pedro was an important place for me as it was where Morey Segal, a fellow union member, transitioned from being a B-man to a successful real estate businessman. I owned an apartment building in San Pedro, which I sold in 2002 before retiring. The area was part of my life both on and off the waterfront.

I met Morey Segal on my first day as a clerk at Matson in 1968. He was a B-man whose career was ending as he moved into real estate in San Pedro, where he became very successful. Morey was a friendly presence during my early days on the waterfront and helped me get acclimated.

During the 1971 ILWU strike, the bosses from Local 94, including Jim North, were involved in providing food and drink to the picketers, which was an unusual but memorable aspect of the strike. Jim North and others were collecting unemployment but still showed up with trucks of food to support the picketers. Local 94 was part of the broader union environment I experienced.

Jim North was one of the bosses from Local 94 during the 1971 strike. Despite the strike, he and others brought trucks of food and drink to the picketers, which helped keep morale high. I remember him as part of the union leadership during that time, even though they were collecting unemployment.

entity: 1971 International Longshoremen's and Warehousemen's Union strike
summary: I participated fully in the 1971 ILWU strike, doing picket duty at Berth 146 in Wilmington. The strike was a unique experience; we had a small shack for food and barbecues, and a big Hawaiian man named Soonac Wasimi would come by to play my ukulele. The bosses even brought us food trucks, and we played football in the street to pass the time. Economically, most of us managed well, with my wife working and me picking up occasional jobs.

entity: 2002 International Longshore and Warehouse Union lockout
summary: I retired on August 1, 2002, just before the ILWU lockout that year. Although I was on crutches from a torn Achilles tendon, I went down to visit the picket line. Shortly after, I drove to Denver, Colorado, so I wasn't deeply involved in the lockout itself. My retirement was timed with the sale of my apartment building in San Pedro.

In 2002, shortly after retiring and visiting the ILWU picket line while on crutches, I drove to Denver, Colorado. This trip coincided with the ILWU lockout, so I was away during much of that event. Denver was a place I went to for a break after my long career on the waterfront.

I wrote my first poem in the early 1970s about the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad that ran from Colfax to Nevada City, California. As a child growing up near Grass Valley, I often saw the little train passing through the small towns. The railroad and the surrounding area inspired my poetry and memories of Northern California.

Colfax, California, was one of the small towns connected by the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad, which I often saw as a child. It was part of the Northern California region where I grew up, near Grass Valley and Nevada City. The railroad and these towns inspired my early poetry in the 1970s.

Nevada City, California, was near my hometown of Grass Valley and along the route of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. I have vivid memories of seeing the train as a child and later wrote a poem about it in the early 1970s. The town and its surroundings are part of my Northern California roots.
